_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
min_doctor_qualification: MS/ DNB/ PG Diploma or equivalent (in ENT)
clinical_indications_uncertain: The document defines the condition and lists symptoms but does not explicitly state clinical indications for hospitalization.

preauth.label: Clinical notes detailing infectious history or sickness contact and admission notes showing vitals and examination findings

claim.label: Relevant investigations report - Laryngoscopy/Nasal endoscopy report

ppd_preauth.q: Were patient's history, physical examination findings and Larygoscopy report suggestive of the diagnosis?

common_queries: Laryngoscopy report not submitted or findings not supportive of diagnosis
Clinical notes missing infectious history, sickness contact, or vitals/examination findings at pre-authorization
Patient's history, physical examination, or laryngoscopy report not suggestive of Severe Acute Laryngitis requiring hospitalization
Relevant investigations report (Laryngoscopy/Nasal endoscopy) not provided during claim submission
Detailed indoor case papers (ICPs) missing or incomplete at claim submission

clinical_key_pointers: Acute laryngitis is a short-term inflammatory condition affecting the larynx or voice box, typically lasting less than 3 weeks; if it lasts over 3 weeks, it is termed chronic laryngitis.

Etiology can be infectious (viral upper respiratory infection agents like rhinovirus, parainfluenza virus, respiratory syncytial virus, coronavirus, adenovirus, influenza; bacterial agents like Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Moraxella catarrhalis) or non-infectious (vocal trauma/abuse/misuse, allergy, gastroesophageal reflux disease, asthma, environmental pollution, smoking, inhalational injuries, or functional/conversion disorders).

In children, croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) is due to parainfluenza virus (most commonly parainfluenza-1), presenting with isolated vocal symptoms, a characteristic "barking" cough, and potentially progressing to inspiratory or biphasic stridor.
Common symptoms include dysphonia (hoarseness), fever (during the first few days), sore throat, dry cough, and frequent throat-clearing.

Management involves Voice Rest, Steam Inhalation, Avoidance of Irritants, Dietary Modification, and Medications such as Antibiotics, Antifungal agents, Anti-reflux medications for Laryngopharyngeal reflux (LPR), H2 receptor and proton pump blocking agents for Gastroesophageal reflux, and Mucolytics for clearing secretions.
